chapters.title: A Letter to God
topics: Author: G.L. Fuentes
Lencho — a simple farmer with unshakable faith in God
Hailstorm destroys crops → writes a letter to God asking for 100 pesos
Postmaster collects money from colleagues → sends 70 pesos
Lencho thinks post office employees stole 30 pesos
Theme: Ironical faith — the very people who helped him are called thieves
importantQuestions: Why did Lencho write a letter to God?
How did the postmaster help Lencho?
Why was Lencho angry after receiving the money?
What is the irony in the story 'A Letter to God'?
Describe Lencho's character in brief.
tips: Irony samjho: Lencho ke faith mein twist hai — jo help kiya unko chor bola
Character sketch questions common hain — Lencho aur Postmaster dono ke liye prepare karo
Extract-based questions mein pehle context identify karo, phir answer likho

chapters.title: Nelson Mandela: Long Walk to Freedom
topics: Author: Nelson Mandela (Autobiography)
10 May 1994 — Inauguration as South Africa's first black President
Apartheid — racial discrimination against non-whites
Mandela spent 27 years in prison on Robben Island
Theme: Courage, sacrifice, and the triumph of the human spirit
Freedom: not just personal freedom but freedom for all
importantQuestions: What does Mandela mean by 'twin obligations'?
Why did he say the oppressor is as much a prisoner as the oppressed?
Describe the inauguration ceremony of Nelson Mandela.
What does courage mean according to Mandela?
How did Mandela's understanding of freedom change over time?
tips: Twin obligations = obligation to family + obligation to people/country
Board mein Mandela ke views on courage aur freedom zaroor poochhe jaate hain
Dates important: 10 May 1994 inauguration, 27 years in prison

chapters.title: Two Stories about Flying
topics: Part 1: 'His First Flight' by Liam O'Flaherty — Young seagull afraid to fly
Family encourages him, mother tricks him with fish → he flies
Theme: Fear can be conquered with courage and trust
Part 2: 'Black Aeroplane' by Frederick Forsyth
Pilot flying to England, encounters storm clouds
Mystery black aeroplane guides him to safety — no one saw it on radar
Theme: Mystery, supernatural help, imagination
importantQuestions: Why was the young seagull afraid to fly?
How did the mother seagull help the young seagull?
What risk did the narrator take in 'Black Aeroplane'?
Who do you think helped the narrator in the Black Aeroplane story?
What is the message of 'His First Flight'?
tips: Dono stories alag hain but ek common theme hai — overcoming fear
'His First Flight' = real story about seagull; 'Black Aeroplane' = mystery fiction
Black Aeroplane ka pilot mystery solved nahi hota — open-ended answer expected in board

chapters.title: From the Diary of Anne Frank
topics: Author: Anne Frank (Diary entries)
Anne, a 13-year-old Jewish girl, writes diary entries during WWII
She names her diary 'Kitty'
Reflects on her feelings, loneliness, and why she writes
Mr Keesing — Maths teacher — punishes her for talking, she writes essays
Theme: Loneliness, importance of writing, adolescent emotions
importantQuestions: Why did Anne Frank call her diary 'Kitty'?
Why did Anne think she could confide more in her diary than in people?
How did Anne handle Mr Keesing's punishment?
What does the diary tell us about the times Anne lived in?
Why does Anne feel she has no true friend?
tips: Anne ki diary WWII ke context mein hai — Holocaust ka background zaroor mention karo
Mr Keesing essays important hain — 3 essays poocha jaata hai (A Chatterbox, Quack Quack Quack, An Incorrigible Chatterbox)
Diary writing ka format bhi yaad rakho — Date, Salutation (Dear Kitty), body, sign off

chapters.title: The Hundred Dresses — I
topics: Author: El Bsor Estes
Wanda Petronski — a Polish girl in an American school
Wears same faded blue dress daily, claims she has 100 dresses at home
Girls tease her (especially Peggy), Maddie feels guilty but stays silent
Theme: Bullying, empathy, courage to stand up
importantQuestions: Why did Wanda claim she had a hundred dresses?
How did Peggy and Maddie react to Wanda's claim?
Why didn't Maddie stop Peggy from teasing Wanda?
What does this story teach us about bullying?
Describe the character of Maddie.
tips: Maddie ka guilt important hai — woh silent bystander thi, peer pressure mein phasi thi
100 dresses actually Wanda ke drawings the — woh art competition win karti hai
Yeh chapter Part I hai — Part II mein resolution aata hai, dono saath prepare karo

chapters.title: The Hundred Dresses — II
topics: Wanda's family moves away; her father writes letter about teasing
Wanda wins the drawing contest — 100 beautiful dress designs
Peggy and Maddie feel remorseful
Maddie resolves never to be a silent spectator again
Wanda gifts her drawings to Peggy and Maddie — faces drawn resemble them
Theme: Resolution, forgiveness, standing up against bullying
importantQuestions: What did Maddie resolve after the incident?
How did the class feel when they came to know that Wanda had won?
What showed that Wanda bore no ill will towards Peggy and Maddie?
Why did the girls write a letter to Wanda?
What lesson does Maddie learn from this experience?
tips: Theme question zaroor aata hai — bullying ke against stand lena, silent spectator na banna
Wanda ke drawings mein Peggy aur Maddie ke faces the — forgiveness ka sign
Maddie ka character transformation most important — guilty → resolve to change

chapters.title: Glimpses of India
topics: Part 1: 'A Baker from Goa' by Lucio Rodrigues — Goan bread-making tradition, the pader
Part 2: 'Coorg' by Lokesh Abrol — Coorg (Kodagu), smallest district of Karnataka, coffee, martial traditions
Part 3: 'Tea from Assam' by Arup Kumar Datta — Tea gardens of Assam, legends of tea origin
Theme: Cultural diversity and richness of India
importantQuestions: What are the important elements of the story 'A Baker from Goa'?
Describe the beauty of Coorg.
What are the legends about the origin of tea?
What role did the baker play in the life of the Goan people?
Why is Coorg called the 'land of rolling hills'?
tips: Teen alag stories hain — har ek ka alag region hai (Goa, Coorg, Assam)
Baker story mein: pader, oven, types of bread (pao, bol, cake) — details important
Coorg facts: smallest district of Karnataka, River Kaveri origin, Kodavus are brave warriors
Tea legends: Chinese emperor (leaves fell in water) + Bodhidharma (cut eyelids grew into tea plant)

chapters.title: Mijbil the Otter
topics: Author: Gavin Maxwell
Maxwell gets an otter (Mijbil/Mij) from Tigris marshes in Iraq
Species: Lutrogale perspicillata maxwelli (new subspecies named after him)
Mij's playful behavior, love for water, football in bathtub
Journey from Iraq to London with Mij — chaos on the plane
Theme: Bond between humans and animals
importantQuestions: Why did Maxwell decide to get an otter as a pet?
Describe Mij's favorite activities.
What happened on the flight from Iraq to London?
How did people in London react to Mij?
What was the new species of otter named after Maxwell?
tips: Otter ki details yaad rakho — species name, where from (Tigris, Iraq)
Plane journey scene — mij escaped from box, scared passengers — exam mein poochha jaata hai
London mein log Mij ko different animals samajhte the — seal? beaver? teddy bear?

chapters.title: Madam Rides the Bus
topics: Author: Vallikkannan
Valli — 8-year-old Tamil girl from a village
Saves money secretly to ride the bus to the town and back
Observes everything on the bus with wonder and curiosity
Sees a dead cow on the return journey — first encounter with death
Theme: Curiosity, independence, reality of life and death
importantQuestions: How did Valli plan her bus journey?
What did Valli see on her way that made her excited?
What was Valli's reaction when she saw the dead cow?
Why didn't Valli want to get off the bus at the town?
What does the story tell us about Valli's character?
tips: Valli 8 saal ki hai par bahut independent aur confident hai — character sketch important
Bus fare 30 paise one way (total 60 paise) — yeh detail board mein poochha jaata hai
Dead cow scene = loss of innocence — pehle live cow dekhi (happy), phir dead cow (sad)

chapters.title: The Sermon at Benares
topics: Gautama Buddha's story — Prince Siddhartha to Buddha
Kisa Gotami — her only son dies, she goes mad with grief
Buddha asks her to bring mustard seeds from a house where no one has died
She realizes death is universal — no one escapes it
Theme: Death is inevitable, attachment causes suffering, acceptance brings peace
importantQuestions: What did Kisa Gotami learn from her search for mustard seeds?
Why did Buddha ask Kisa Gotami to bring mustard seeds from a house that had never experienced death?
What was the sermon that Buddha gave at Benares?
How did Siddhartha Gautama become the Buddha?
What is the message of the story?
tips: Buddha ki teaching ka core: suffering is universal, death se koi bach nahi sakta
Kisa Gotami ka transformation important — grief → wisdom → acceptance
Benares = Varanasi — Buddha ka first sermon yahi tha (Deer Park, Sarnath)

chapters.title: The Proposal
topics: Author: Anton Chekhov (Play/Drama)
Lomov comes to propose to Natalya Stepanovna
But they keep quarreling about: Oxen Meadows (whose land?) and dogs (Guess vs Squeezer)
Lomov has anxiety attacks; Chubukov (Natalya's father) tries to manage
Finally they get engaged — but keep arguing!
Theme: Comedy, satire on marriage and petty disputes
importantQuestions: What was the real purpose of Lomov's visit?
What were the two main arguments between Lomov and Natalya?
Describe the character of Natalya.
How does the play end?
What is the satirical message of 'The Proposal'?
tips: Yeh ek play hai — dialogue-based, format yaad rakho
3 characters: Lomov (nervous), Natalya (argumentative), Chubukov (greedy father)
Dono arguments: (1) Oxen Meadows — land dispute, (2) Dogs — Guess vs Squeezer
Satire samjho: proposal hua but pyaar nahi, sirf jhagda — comedy of manners

chapters.title: Dust of Snow
topics: Poet: Robert Frost
A crow shakes snow dust from a hemlock tree onto the poet
This small incident changes his mood — saves his ruined day
Theme: Nature can heal; small moments can change our perspective
Poetic devices: Alliteration, Enjambment, Symbolism
importantQuestions: What is the significance of the crow and hemlock tree?
How did the dust of snow change the poet's mood?
What does the poem teach us about nature?
Identify the poetic devices used in the poem.
Why does the poet use negative symbols (crow, hemlock) for a positive message?
tips: Crow = sadness, hemlock = poison/death — negative symbols for positive message = CONTRAST
Very short poem (8 lines) — poochha zaroor jaata hai, word by word yaad karo
Poetic devices: Alliteration (has, hemlock), Enjambment (lines flow without pause)

chapters.title: Fire and Ice
topics: Poet: Robert Frost
Fire = desire/passion, Ice = hatred/indifference
Debate: Will the world end in fire or ice?
Both are destructive enough to destroy the world
Theme: Human emotions (desire and hatred) can destroy everything
Poetic devices: Symbolism, Anaphora, Antithesis
importantQuestions: What does 'fire' symbolize in the poem?
What does 'ice' symbolize?
What is the poet's opinion about the end of the world?
Explain: 'I think I know enough of hate / To say that for destruction ice / Is also great'
What is the message of the poem?
tips: Fire = desire (greed, lust) | Ice = hatred (coldness, indifference) — yeh symbolism zaroor likho
Poet says BOTH can destroy — pehle fire, agar dobara chance mile toh ice bhi kaafi hai
Short poem but deep meaning — board mein 5 marks ka question aa sakta hai

chapters.title: A Tiger in the Zoo
topics: Poet: Leslie Norris
Contrast: Tiger in zoo (caged, helpless) vs Tiger in wild (free, majestic)
In zoo: walks in cage, ignores visitors, stares at stars at night
In wild: would lurk in shadow, slide through grass near villages, terrorize people
Theme: Animals should be free, captivity is cruel
Poetic devices: Contrast, Imagery, Personification
importantQuestions: How does the tiger behave in the zoo?
How would the tiger behave in the wild?
What is the main contrast in the poem?
Why does the tiger stare at the stars?
What message does the poet convey about caged animals?
tips: Contrast hi poem ka core hai — zoo (sad, quiet, caged) vs jungle (powerful, free, fierce)
Last stanza important: 'He stares with his brilliant eyes at the brilliant stars' — longing for freedom
Animal rights angle se answer likhna — examiner ko relevant lagta hai

chapters.title: How to Tell Wild Animals
topics: Poet: Carolyn Wells
Humorous poem — describes wild animals by what they do to you!
Asian Lion (roars, you die), Bengal Tiger (eats you, noble), Leopard (spots, peppered)
Bear (hugs you tight), Hyena (laughs), Crocodile (crying while eating)
Theme: Humour, irony — you won't survive to tell the difference!
Poetic devices: Humour, Irony, Rhyme scheme
importantQuestions: How can you identify the Asian Lion?
What is the difference between a tiger and a leopard according to the poem?
Why is the poem humorous?
How does the poet describe a bear?
What is the irony in the poem?
tips: Funny poem! Irony: animals ko identify karne ka tarika = they attack you first
Spot the difference: Tiger = black stripes; Leopard = spots/peppered
Hyena laughs, Crocodile cries (crocodile tears) — yeh details yaad rakho

chapters.title: The Ball Poem
topics: Poet: John Berryman
A boy loses his ball in the water (harbour)
First experience of loss — money can't buy back the same ball
Boy must learn to cope with loss on his own
Theme: Learning to deal with loss, growing up, epistemology of loss
Poetic devices: Enjambment, Metaphor, Imagery, Alliteration
importantQuestions: Why does the poet say 'No use to say O there are other balls'?
What does the loss of the ball teach the boy?
What is the 'epistemology of loss'?
Why doesn't the poet offer to buy another ball?
How is the ball a metaphor for life?
tips: Epistemology of loss = learning how to deal with loss — yeh term zaroor use karo answer mein
Ball = innocence, childhood memories — money se wapas nahi aa sakti
Poet deliberately help nahi karta — kyunki boy ko khud seekhna hai cope karna

chapters.title: Amanda!
topics: Poet: Robin Klein
Amanda — a girl constantly being nagged by her mother for everything
Amanda imagines herself as: Mermaid (freedom in sea), Rapunzel (alone in tower), orphan (with no one to nag)
Alternating stanzas: reality (mom nagging) and fantasy (Amanda's escape)
Theme: Excessive control stifles creativity; children need freedom
Poetic devices: Rhyme scheme, Repetition, Imagery
importantQuestions: Why does Amanda imagine herself as a mermaid?
What kind of life does Amanda wish for?
How does the mother's nagging affect Amanda?
Why does Amanda imagine herself as an orphan?
What is the message of the poem?
tips: Italics waale stanzas = Amanda ka imagination; normal = mom nagging
3 fantasy: Mermaid (sea freedom), Rapunzel (tower peace), Orphan (no parents = no nagging)
Rapunzel mein Amanda says 'I will never let down my hair' — means she doesn't want anyone to reach her

chapters.title: Animals
topics: Poet: Walt Whitman
Poet prefers animals over humans — animals are calm, self-contained, don't whine
Humans have lost their good qualities; animals still possess them (tokens of the poet's earlier self)
Animals: don't complain, don't lie awake in the dark, don't weep for sins, not obsessed with possessions
Theme: Criticism of human nature; animals represent lost innocence
Poetic devices: Anaphora, Repetition, Contrast
importantQuestions: Why does the poet want to live with animals?
What are the qualities of animals that the poet admires?
What has the poet lost that animals still have?
What does 'tokens' mean in the poem?
How does the poet contrast humans with animals?
tips: Whitman ke andar jealousy hai — humans ne good qualities khoi hain jo animals mein abhi bhi hain
'Tokens' = signs/evidence of goodness that humans had earlier
Anaphora: 'They do not...' repeated — exam mein poetic device zaroor poochha jaata hai

chapters.title: The Trees
topics: Poet: Adrienne Rich
Trees inside a glass house/greenhouse are moving outside into the forest
Symbolism: Trees = women, glass house = patriarchal society
Trees breaking free = women breaking free from oppression
Nature imagery: roots work all night, branches push against the roof
Theme: Freedom, breaking barriers, women's liberation
Poetic devices: Symbolism, Imagery, Personification
importantQuestions: What do the trees symbolize in the poem?
How are the trees moving out of the glass house?
What is the deeper meaning of 'The Trees'?
What does the glass house represent?
Describe the imagery in the poem.
tips: Surface level: trees moving from greenhouse to forest | Deep level: women's liberation
Glass house = patriarchy/restrictions; Trees going outside = breaking free
Adrienne Rich is a feminist poet — yeh context answer mein mention karo

chapters.title: Fog
topics: Poet: Carl Sandburg
Short imagist poem — fog compared to a cat
Fog comes 'on little cat feet', sits and looks over the city, moves on
Extended metaphor: fog's movement = cat's behaviour (silent, quiet, temporary)
Theme: Nature's beauty in small things; transience
Poetic devices: Extended Metaphor, Personification, Imagery
importantQuestions: How does the poet compare fog to a cat?
What are the similarities between the fog and a cat?
What poetic device is used throughout the poem?
What is the theme of the poem 'Fog'?
Describe the movement of the fog.
tips: Sirf 6 lines ka poem — extended metaphor samjho (fog = cat throughout)
3 similarities: silent arrival, sitting and watching, quietly moving away
Board mein mostly poetic device aur comparison poocha jaata hai — METAPHOR likho, simile nahi

chapters.title: The Tale of Custard the Dragon
topics: Poet: Ogden Nash
Characters: Belinda (girl), Custard (cowardly dragon), Ink (kitten), Blink (mouse), Mustard (dog)
Everyone is brave except Custard who is a coward — everyone mocks him
Pirate attacks → Custard fights and eats the pirate, saves everyone!
After victory, others again claim they would have been braver
Theme: True courage vs boasting; appearances are deceptive
Poetic devices: Humour, Irony, Rhyme, Ballad form
importantQuestions: Why was Custard considered a coward?
How did Custard prove his bravery?
What is the irony in the poem?
How did others react after Custard killed the pirate?
What is the message of the poem about true courage?
tips: Irony: sabse 'coward' = sabse brave nikla (Custard); so-called 'brave' ones hid
Ballad form hai — story poem, rhyming couplets mein
Names yaad rakho: Ink (kitten, black), Blink (mouse), Mustard (dog, yellow mustard)
Board mein character comparison aata hai — Custard vs others

chapters.title: For Anne Gregory
topics: Poet: W.B. Yeats
Dialogue between the poet and a young woman (Anne Gregory)
Young men love her for her beautiful yellow hair, not for herself
Anne says she can dye her hair — poet says they still won't love her true self
Only God can love someone for themselves alone — not for external beauty
Theme: Inner beauty vs outer beauty; true love
Poetic devices: Dialogue form, Rhyme, Alliteration
importantQuestions: What is the main argument between the poet and Anne Gregory?
Why does Anne want to dye her hair?
What does the poet mean by 'only God could love you for yourself alone'?
What is the theme of the poem?
Do you agree that we should be loved for who we are? Why?
tips: Poem conversation format mein hai — 2 viewpoints: poet (reality) vs Anne (idealism)
Yellow hair = external beauty; Anne wants to be loved for who she is inside
Last line ka reference: religious text — only God loves unconditionally

chapters.title: A Triumph of Surgery
topics: Author: James Herriot
Mrs Pumphrey's dog Tricki is overfed and falls sick
Dr Herriot takes Tricki to his surgery, puts him on a strict diet
Tricki recovers fully, Mrs Pumphrey sends gifts (eggs, wine) thinking they're for Tricki
Dr Herriot and partners enjoy the luxury food!
Theme: Overindulgence is harmful; tough love works
importantQuestions: Why was Dr Herriot worried about Tricki?
How did Dr Herriot treat Tricki?
What was Mrs Pumphrey's reaction?
Do you think Mrs Pumphrey was a good pet owner? Why/Why not?
Why is the story called 'A Triumph of Surgery'?
tips: Tricki ki bimari = overeating (Mrs Pumphrey bahut khilati thi)
Dr Herriot ne koi surgery nahi ki — sirf diet control kiya — isliye 'Triumph of Surgery' ironic title hai
Mrs Pumphrey ka love = overindulgence — useful example for real life answers

chapters.title: The Thief's Story
topics: Author: Ruskin Bond
Hari Singh — a 15-year-old thief — befriends Anil (a writer)
Anil trusts Hari, teaches him to read and write, gives him affection
Hari steals ₹600 from Anil but returns it — guilt and trust change him
Theme: Trust and affection can reform a person
importantQuestions: Why did Hari Singh return the stolen money?
How did Anil treat Hari Singh?
What made Hari Singh change his ways?
Why did Hari Singh not board the train?
What role did education play in Hari's transformation?
tips: Hari ne paisa return kiya kyunki: (1) guilt, (2) Anil ka trust, (3) education ka opportunity lose nahi karna tha
Anil KNEW Hari stole — wet notes feel kiya — but kuch nahi bola | Trust ka example
Education as empowerment — yeh theme board mein important hai

chapters.title: Footprints Without Feet
topics: Author: H.G. Wells
Griffin — a brilliant scientist who discovers how to make himself invisible
Uses invisibility for crime: sets landlord's house on fire, steals from shop, attacks people
Takes shelter at Mrs Hall's inn in Iping village, acts suspiciously
Finally caught when bandages removed revealing nothing underneath
Theme: Science without ethics is dangerous; genius can become criminal
importantQuestions: How did Griffin become invisible?
Why did Griffin set the house on fire?
How did Griffin's invisibility lead to his downfall?
What kind of person was Griffin?
What is the message of the story?
tips: Griffin = brilliant scientist but MISUSES his discovery — science without conscience
Title ka meaning: invisible person ke footprints dikh rahe the (mud mein)
Mrs Hall ke inn ka scene aur constable Jaffers ka scene — important for extracts

chapters.title: The Making of a Scientist
topics: Author: Robert W. Peterson
Richard Ebright — a curious child who became a famous scientist
Started collecting butterflies at age 3, won county science fair
Research: How cells read their DNA → discovered a new hormone in monarch butterflies
Key qualities: curiosity, hard work, keen observation, methodical approach
Theme: Curiosity and dedication make a true scientist
importantQuestions: How did Richard Ebright's interest in butterflies lead to his scientific career?
What qualities made Ebright a successful scientist?
What was Ebright's significant discovery?
How did his mother encourage his scientific interests?
What role did 'The Travels of Monarch X' play in Ebright's life?
tips: Ebright ka journey: butterflies → tagging experiment → cells/DNA → gold plate model
Mother's role bahut important — encourage kiya, books laati thi (especially 'Travels of Monarch X')
3 qualities: curiosity + hard work + first-rate mind — board mein zaroor likho

chapters.title: Necklace (The Necklace)
topics: Author: Guy de Maupassant
Matilda Loisel borrows a diamond necklace from her rich friend Mme Forestier for a party
She loses the necklace, replaces it with a real one costing ₹36,000 francs
Works for 10 years to pay off the debt
Shocking twist: the original necklace was FAKE (worth only 500 francs)
Theme: Vanity leads to ruin; appearances are deceptive; irony of fate
importantQuestions: Why did Matilda borrow the necklace?
What happened at the party?
How did the Loisels pay for the replacement necklace?
What is the twist at the end of the story?
What is the moral of 'The Necklace'?
tips: TWIST ending = original necklace NAQLI thi — yeh irony ka perfect example
10 saal ki mehnat ek fake necklace ke liye — vanity ki sazaa
Board mein moral question zaroor aata hai — honesty, contentment, don't pretend to be what you're not

chapters.title: The Hack Driver
topics: Author: Sinclair Lewis
Young lawyer sent to serve summons on Oliver Lutkins in New Mullion village
Bill Magnuson (hack driver) = actually Oliver Lutkins himself!
Bill takes the lawyer around town, prevents him from finding Lutkins (himself)
Lawyer goes back to city, returns with another person who identifies Lutkins = Bill
Theme: Appearance vs reality; gullibility; small-town cunning
importantQuestions: Why was the lawyer sent to New Mullion?
How did the hack driver deceive the lawyer?
What impression did the lawyer form of Bill?
How did the lawyer discover the truth about Bill?
What does the story say about trusting strangers?
tips: Bill = Lutkins — same person! Lawyer itna naive tha ki pata hi nahi chala
Bill ne lawyer ko pura gaon ghuma diya — bahut smart deception
Second visit mein colleague ne identify kiya — tab lawyer shame feel kiya
Appearance vs Reality — yeh theme har answer mein mention karo

chapters.title: Bholi
topics: Author: K.A. Abbas
Bholi (Sulekha) — born simple, scarred by pox, stammers, considered dull
Parents neglect her; sent to school as an afterthought
Teacher encourages her — transforms her confidence
On wedding day, groom Bishamber demands dowry → Bholi refuses to marry him
Theme: Education empowers; don't judge by appearances; women's empowerment
importantQuestions: How did Bholi's teacher change her life?
Why did Bholi refuse to marry Bishamber?
What kind of person was Bholi's father?
How is 'Bholi' a story of women's empowerment?
What is the role of education in Bholi's transformation?
tips: Teacher ka role highlight karo — pehli baar kisi ne Bholi ko respect diya
Bishamber ₹5000 dowry maangta hai — Bholi refuse karti hai = empowerment moment
Education = transformation — stammering girl → confident, outspoken woman
Board mein character sketch aur long answer dono aate hain

chapters.title: Tenses
topics: Present Tense: Simple, Continuous, Perfect, Perfect Continuous
Past Tense: Simple, Continuous, Perfect, Perfect Continuous
Future Tense: Simple (will/shall), Continuous, Perfect, Perfect Continuous
Total 12 tenses — structure and usage
rules: Simple Present: S + V1(s/es) + O — 'She writes daily'
Present Continuous: S + is/am/are + V-ing — 'She is writing now'
Present Perfect: S + has/have + V3 — 'She has written the letter'
Simple Past: S + V2 + O — 'She wrote yesterday'
Past Continuous: S + was/were + V-ing — 'She was writing'
Past Perfect: S + had + V3 — 'She had written before he came'
Simple Future: S + will/shall + V1 — 'She will write tomorrow'
examples: I go to school daily. (Simple Present)
She is reading a book right now. (Present Continuous)
They have finished their homework. (Present Perfect)
He played football yesterday. (Simple Past)
We were watching TV when the bell rang. (Past Continuous)
She had already left before I arrived. (Past Perfect)
I will call you tomorrow. (Simple Future)
tips: Tense identify karne ke liye TIME MARKERS dekho: daily/always = Simple Present, now = Continuous, already/just = Perfect, yesterday = Past
Board mein gap-filling aur transformation dono aate hain — practice karo
Past Perfect tab use hota hai jab 2 past actions mein pehle wala batana ho

chapters.title: Active and Passive Voice
topics: Active Voice: Subject performs the action
Passive Voice: Subject receives the action
Conversion rules for all tenses
Special cases: Modals, Imperative sentences, Questions
rules: Active: S + V + O → Passive: O + be + V3 + by S
Simple Present: writes → is written
Simple Past: wrote → was written
Present Continuous: is writing → is being written
Past Continuous: was writing → was being written
Present Perfect: has written → has been written
Past Perfect: had written → had been written
Modal: can write → can be written
Future: will write → will be written
examples: Active: She writes a letter. → Passive: A letter is written by her.
Active: He made a cake. → Passive: A cake was made by him.
Active: They are building a house. → Passive: A house is being built by them.
Active: She can solve this. → Passive: This can be solved by her.
Active: Open the door. → Passive: Let the door be opened.
tips: Step 1: Object ko aage lao, Step 2: 'be' form add karo (tense ke hisaab se), Step 3: V3 use karo, Step 4: 'by + subject'
Modals mein: modal + be + V3 (can be done, should be done, etc.)
Imperative: Let + object + be + V3 (Let it be done)

chapters.title: Reported Speech (Indirect Speech)
topics: Direct to Indirect Speech conversion
Changes in: Tense, Pronouns, Time/Place expressions
Reporting verbs: said, told, asked, ordered, requested
Types: Statements, Questions, Commands/Requests, Exclamations
rules: Present → Past (He said, 'I am happy' → He said that he was happy)
Past → Past Perfect (She said, 'I went' → She said she had gone)
will → would, can → could, may → might
this → that, here → there, now → then, today → that day, tomorrow → the next day, yesterday → the previous day
Questions: asked + if/whether (Yes/No) or asked + wh-word
Commands: ordered/requested + to + V1
Exclamations: exclaimed + that
examples: Direct: He said, 'I am tired.' → Indirect: He said that he was tired.
Direct: She said, 'I will come.' → Indirect: She said that she would come.
Direct: 'Are you happy?' he asked. → Indirect: He asked if I was happy.
Direct: 'Where do you live?' she asked. → Indirect: She asked where I lived.
Direct: 'Sit down,' the teacher said. → Indirect: The teacher ordered them to sit down.
tips: Sabse pehle reporting verb dekho (said/asked/ordered) — isse type pata chalega
Tense ek step BACK jaata hai: Present→Past, Past→Past Perfect, Will→Would
Pronouns change hote hain — speaker ke perspective se likho
Agar reporting verb PRESENT tense mein hai, toh tense change NAHI hota

chapters.title: Letter Writing
topics: Formal Letter: Complaint, Application, Editor, Enquiry
Informal Letter: To friend, relative
Format: Sender's address, Date, Receiver's address, Subject, Salutation, Body, Closing
rules: Formal format: Sender's address (top left) → Date → Receiver's address → Subject → Sir/Madam → Body (3 paras) → Yours faithfully → Name
Informal format: Address → Date → Dear [Name] → Body → Yours lovingly → Name
Body: Para 1 = Introduction/Purpose, Para 2 = Details, Para 3 = Conclusion/Request
Word limit: 100-120 words
Subject line must be clear and brief
examples: Formal: Letter to Editor about pollution in your area
Formal: Letter to Principal requesting leave/organizing an event
Formal: Complaint letter about water supply/road conditions
Informal: Letter to friend inviting for birthday/vacation
tips: FORMAT = 2 marks — galat format = marks cut! Pehle format practice karo
Formal mein: Yours faithfully (if Sir/Madam); Yours sincerely (if name known)
Body mein 3 paragraphs zaroor likho — single block mat likho
Subject line mein: 'Regarding...' ya 'Sub: ...' likho — crisp and clear

chapters.title: Article & Paragraph Writing
topics: Article: Title, By-line, Introduction, Body, Conclusion
Common topics: Pollution, Social Media, Education, Health, Festivals
Paragraph Writing: Single topic, clear structure
rules: Article format: TITLE (catchy) → By: [Your Name] → Introduction → Body (2-3 paras with facts/examples) → Conclusion
Word limit: 150-200 words
Use facts, statistics, quotes to make it engaging
Paragraph: Topic sentence → Supporting sentences → Concluding sentence
examples: Article: 'Importance of Trees in Urban Areas'
Article: 'Impact of Social Media on Students'
Article: 'Online Education: Boon or Bane?'
Paragraph: 'My Favorite Festival'
tips: Title catchy hona chahiye — question ya quote se start karo
By-line zaroor likho: 'By: [Name]' — marks milte hain format ke
Introduction mein hook do: shocking fact ya question
Conclusion mein suggestion/appeal likhne se acche marks aate hain

chapters.title: Reading Comprehension
topics: Unseen Passage: Factual and Discursive
Types of questions: MCQ, Short answer, Vocabulary-based, Title suggestion
Skills tested: Main idea, inference, vocabulary in context
rules: Read passage TWICE — first for overview, second for details
Answer in your own words unless asked to quote
Vocabulary questions: understand from context, not memorized meaning
Title should reflect the MAIN IDEA, not a detail
tips: Pehle questions padho, PHIR passage padho — targeted reading saves time
Answer passage mein hi hota hai — apne se mat likho, passage se uthaao
Vocabulary question mein: sentence mein word ka meaning context se samjho
Title question last mein karo — pura passage samajhne ke baad title do
Word limit follow karo — zyada likhne se marks nahi katate but time waste hota hai
